examples.title: Truth Table Generator for Two Variables
examples.description: Generates complete truth table for any compound proposition with 2 variables
examples.code:
ALGORITHM GenerateTruthTable2Vars
BEGIN
 DECLARE variables = ["p", "q"]
 DECLARE operators = ["AND", "OR", "IMPLIES", "IFF"]
 
 PRINT "Truth Table for 2 Variables:"
 PRINT "p	q"
 PRINT "-	-"
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO 3 DO
 p = (i / 2) mod 2 // 0 or 1
 q = i mod 2 // 0 or 1
 PRINT p, q
 END FOR
 
 // Output order: 00, 01, 10, 11
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm generates all 4 combinations of truth values for 2 propositional variables in systematic binary order: p=F,q=F; p=F,q=T; p=T,q=F; p=T,q=T.

examples.title: Evaluate Logical Connectives
examples.description: Functions to evaluate basic logical connectives given boolean inputs
examples.code:
FUNCTION Neg(p)
 RETURN NOT p
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION And(p, q)
 RETURN p AND q
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION Or(p, q)
 RETURN p OR q
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION Implies(p, q)
 // p→q is FALSE only when p=TRUE and q=FALSE
 IF p = TRUE AND q = FALSE THEN
 RETURN FALSE
 ELSE
 RETURN TRUE
 END IF
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION Iff(p, q)
 // p↔q is TRUE when both have same value
 IF p = q THEN
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Each function implements the truth table definition for the corresponding connective. The Implies function is particularly important as it differs from simple boolean operations.

examples.title: Check Tautology or Contradiction
examples.description: Algorithm to determine if a compound proposition is tautology, contradiction, or contingency
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ClassifyProposition(prop, n)
BEGIN
 DECLARE resultList = []
 numRows = 2^n
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO numRows-1 DO
 // Generate truth values for n variables
 values = GetBinaryValues(i, n)
 result = Evaluate(prop, values)
 APPEND result TO resultList
 END FOR
 
 allTrue = TRUE
 allFalse = TRUE
 
 FOR EACH val IN resultList DO
 IF val = FALSE THEN allTrue = FALSE
 IF val = TRUE THEN allFalse = FALSE
 END FOR
 
 IF allTrue THEN
 RETURN "Tautology"
 ELSE IF allFalse THEN
 RETURN "Contradiction"
 ELSE
 RETURN "Contingency"
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm evaluates a proposition for all possible combinations and classifies it based on the results: all true = tautology, all false = contradiction, mixed = contingency.

examples.title: Verify Logical Equivalence
examples.description: Algorithm to check if two propositions are logically equivalent using truth tables
examples.code:
ALGORITHM CheckLogicalEquivalence(prop1, prop2, n)
BEGIN
 numRows = 2^n
 equivalent = TRUE
 
 FOR i FROM 0 TO numRows-1 DO
 values = GetBinaryValues(i, n)
 result1 = Evaluate(prop1, values)
 result2 = Evaluate(prop2, values)
 
 IF result1 != result2 THEN
 equivalent = FALSE
 BREAK
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 IF equivalent THEN
 PRINT prop1 + " ≡ " + prop2
 PRINT "They are logically equivalent!"
 ELSE
 PRINT prop1 + " ≠ " + prop2
 PRINT "They are NOT logically equivalent."
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: Two propositions are logically equivalent if they have the same truth value for every possible combination of input values. This algorithm systematically checks all combinations.
